note: The coarse legal character every operative record carries, and the single thing the extractor must get right: may (power) vs must (duty) vs block (veto). It MUST agree with the record type (a schema check: a Power record carries modality=power, etc.). Active from Spiral 2. v0.2 (2026-07-20) adds definitions and the boundary traps.
boundary_traps: 'must be consulted' is a DUTY owed by the consulting actor, not a veto held by the consultee. The consultee's modality is not veto merely because their view is inconvenient to ignore.
'must have regard to' creates a duty to consider, not an obligation to comply, and confers nothing on the body regarded. It is not recorded as a veto at all.

'may, with the consent of X' contains TWO records: a power held by the actor (modality=power) and a veto held by X (modality=veto, derived from that power). One provision, one canonical record - the veto is the derived projection.
A power expressed as a discretion the holder is in practice always expected to exercise is still a power. Grade the law, not the convention.

terms.definition: A legal capability the holder MAY exercise at their discretion. The holder chooses whether to act; not acting is lawful.
terms.test: Could the holder lawfully decline to do this? If yes, it is a power.

terms.definition: An obligation the holder MUST discharge. Not acting is a breach.
terms.test: Could the holder lawfully decline to do this? If no, it is a duty. Note that 'shall' and 'must' both create duties; Office of the Parliamentary Counsel drafting policy is to avoid the legislative 'shall', so older Acts use it where newer ones say 'must'.

terms.definition: The blocking projection of a power: the holder's decision determines whether ANOTHER actor may lawfully proceed. Never a primitive - always derived from a canonical Power record, and always carrying the decision it blocks.
terms.test: Name the actor blocked and the decision blocked. If you cannot name both, it is not a veto record.
